# Tools

## Data analysis tools and pipelines

Many tools, programs, pipelines, and software suites are used in the analysis of ancient biomolecules. Below are a collection of commonly used software resources, including with links for their download. If you have add additional resources to suggest, please send your suggestions to webmaster@isbarch.org.

### Ancient DNA

**Multipurpose GUI platforms**

**Initial data handling and quality assessment**

**DNA sequence alignment**

**DNA sequence assembly**

**Ancient DNA damage**

**Variant calling and genotyping**

**Sex, kinship, and contamination**

**Ancestry and admixture**

**Epigenetics**

**Population and evolutionary history**

**Species identification and metagenomics**

**Visualization tools**

--- SOURCE 3: eager: Introduction - nf-core ---

FULL CONTENT:
# nf-core/eager [Edit](https://github.com/nf-core/eager/blob/2.3.3/README.md "Edit this page on GitHub")

A fully reproducible and state-of-the-art ancient DNA analysis pipeline

`2.3.3`
 `2.5.3` 
`22.10.6`

## Introduction

**nf-core/eager** is a bioinformatics best-practise analysis pipeline for NGS sequencing based ancient DNA (aDNA) data analysis.

The pipeline is built using [Nextflow](https://www.nextflow.io/), a workflow tool to run tasks across multiple compute infrastructures in a very portable manner. It comes with docker containers making installation trivial and results highly reproducible. The pipeline pre-processes raw data from FASTQ inputs, or preprocessed BAM inputs. It can align reads and performs extensive general NGS and aDNA specific quality-control on the results. It comes with docker, singularity or conda containers making installation trivial and results highly reproducible.

![nf-core/eager schematic workflow](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nf-core/eager/2.3.3//docs/images/usage/eager2_workflow.png)

![nf-core/eager schematic workflow](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nf-core/eager/2.3.3//docs/images/usage/eager2_workflow.png)

## Quick Start

Install [`nextflow`](https://nf-co.re/usage/installation) (version >= 20.04.0)

`nextflow`

Install any of [`Docker`](https://docs.docker.com/engine/installation/), [`Singularity`](https://www.sylabs.io/guides/3.0/user-guide/), [`Podman`](https://podman.io/), [`Shifter`](https://nersc.gitlab.io/development/shifter/how-to-use/) or [`Charliecloud`](https://hpc.github.io/charliecloud/) for full pipeline reproducibility *(please only use [`Conda`](https://conda.io/miniconda.html) as a last resort; see [docs](https://nf-co.re/usage/configuration#basic-configuration-profiles))*

`Docker`
`Singularity`
`Podman`
`Shifter`
`Charliecloud`
`Conda`

Download the pipeline and test it on a minimal dataset with a single command:

`nextflow run nf-core/eager -profile test_tsv,<docker/singularity/podman/shifter/charliecloud/conda/institute>`

Please check [nf-core/configs](https://github.com/nf-core/configs#documentation) to see if a custom config file to run nf-core pipelines already exists for your Institute. If so, you can simply use `-profile <institute>` in your command. This will enable either `docker` or `singularity` and set the appropriate execution settings for your local compute environment.

`-profile <institute>`
`docker`
`singularity`

Start running your own analysis!

`nextflow run nf-core/eager -profile <docker/singularity/podman/conda/institute> --input '*_R{1,2}.fastq.gz' --fasta '<your_reference>.fasta'`

Once your run has completed successfully, clean up the intermediate files.

`nextflow clean -f -k`

See [usage docs](https://nf-co.re/eager/docs/usage/) for all of the available options when running the pipeline.

**N.B.** You can see an overview of the run in the MultiQC report located at `./results/MultiQC/multiqc_report.html`

`./results/MultiQC/multiqc_report.html`

Modifications to the default pipeline are easily made using various options as described in the documentation.

## Pipeline Summary

### Default Steps

By default the pipeline currently performs the following:

`bwa`
`samtools`
`picard`
`FastQC`
`AdapterRemoval`
`bwa aln`
`bwa mem`
`CircularMapper`
`bowtie2`
`samtools`
`DamageProfiler`
`DeDup`
`MarkDuplicates`
`Qualimap`
`preseq`
`MultiQC`

### Additional Steps

Additional functionality contained by the pipeline currently includes:

#### Input

#### Preprocessing

`fastp`
`samtools`

#### aDNA Damage manipulation

`BamUtil`
`PMDTools`
`angsd`

#### Genotyping

`GATK UnifiedGenotyper`
`GATK HaplotypeCaller`
`FreeBayes`
`pileupCaller`
`angsd`
`VCF2Genome`
`MultiVCFAnalyzer`

#### Biological Information

`MtNucRatioCalculator`
`Sex.DetERRmine`

#### Metagenomic Screening

`BBduk`
`MALT`
`Kraken2`
`MaltExtract`

#### Functionality Overview

A graphical overview of suggested routes through the pipeline depending on context can be seen below.

![nf-core/eager metro map](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nf-core/eager/2.3.3//docs/images/usage/eager2_metromap_complex.png)

![nf-core/eager metro map](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/nf-core/eager/2.3.3//docs/images/usage/eager2_metromap_complex.png)

## Documentation

The nf-core/eager pipeline comes with documentation about the pipeline: [usage](https://nf-co.re/eager/usage) and [output](https://nf-co.re/eager/output).

## Contributions and Support

If you would like to contribute to this pipeline, please see the [contributing guidelines](https://github.com/nf-core/eager/blob/2.3.3/.github/CONTRIBUTING.md).

For further information or help, don’t hesitate to get in touch on the [Slack `#eager` channel](https://nfcore.slack.com/channels/eager) (you can join with [this invite](https://nf-co.re/join/slack)).

`#eager`

## Citations

If you use `nf-core/eager` for your analysis, please cite the `eager` preprint as follows:

`nf-core/eager`
`eager`

Fellows Yates JA, Lamnidis TC, Borry M, Valtueña Andrades A, Fagernäs Z, Clayton S, Garcia MU, Neukamm J, Peltzer A. 2021. Reproducible, portable, and efficient ancient genome reconstruction with nf-core/eager. PeerJ 9:e10947. DOI: [10.7717/peerj.10947](https://doi.org/10.7717/peerj.10947).

You can cite the eager zenodo record for a specific version using the following [doi: 10.5281/zenodo.3698082](https://zenodo.org/badge/latestdoi/135918251)

You can cite the `nf-core` publication as follows:

`nf-core`

Philip Ewels, Alexander Peltzer, Sven Fillinger, Harshil Patel, Johannes Alneberg, Andreas Wilm, Maxime Ulysse Garcia, Paolo Di Tommaso & Sven Nahnsen.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Bioinformatics tools like BWA, Bowtie2, and nf-core/eager streamline ancient DNA analysis by aligning sequences to reference genomes, ensuring accurate data handling and quality assessment.
Interpreting genetic data involves using algorithms for variant calling and assessing genotype likelihoods, enabling researchers to extract meaningful insights from ancient DNA samples.

Linking genetic findings to archaeological context enhances understanding of human evolution and migration, integrating bioinformatics results with historical and cultural data for comprehensive analysis.
